coredata.dc:title: Cu-crosslinked carboxymethylcellulose/naproxen/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel beads for naproxen oral delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Javanbakht, Siamak
Nazari, Naser
Rakhshaei, Rasul
Namazi, Hassan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, copper acetate was used as a new physical crosslinker to prepare carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) based hydrogel nanocomposite beads. Due to the characteristics of the prepared CMC-based hydrogel nanocomposite beads such as mildness, simplicity, and the creation of small and uniform shapes, the presented procedure could attract great consideration in the field of controlled release of drugs. Naproxen (NPX) as a model drug was pre-loaded during the preparation of hydrogel beads. The prepared Cu-crosslinked carboxymethylcellulose/NPX/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel beads (Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD) characterized using FT-IR, Zeta potential, DSC and SEM analysis methods In order to demonstrate the efficiency of the prepared nanocomposite beads as a controlled drug delivery system, the drug delivery tests carried out in the gastrointestinal tract simulated conditions. The resulted drug release analysis showed that CMC could effectively protect the loaded drug against stomach pH. With controlling the releases in the gastrointestinal tract conditions, the long-term stability of drug dosing enhanced. The MTT test demonstrated that the hydrogel nanocomposite beads have low toxicity against Caco-2 cells. The obtained results showed that the prepared beads could be a choice for drug capsule in the gastrointestinal tract conditions; furthermore, it potentially could be used as an oral drug delivery system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Graphene quantum dot
Oral delivery
Naproxen

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GQDs: SEM (a), DLS of GQDs (b), UV–Vis absorption spectra of GQDs in water (c), EDX analysis for the prepared GQDs (d), Photoluminescence spectrum of the dilute aqueous solution of GQDs and the photograph demonstrates the fluorescence of a dilute aqueous solution of GQDs(e).
FT-IR spectra for the CA, GQDs, pure CMC, NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD.
DSC thermograms for the pure CMC and Cu-CMC beads (a) and Zeta potentials of the Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD (b).
SEM images of the (a) Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD, (b) 10%, (c) 20%, (d) 30% GQDs content (insets are higher magnifications).
Swelling behavior of Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD with different GQD concentrations in simulated gastrointestinal tract passage (pH and time).
Drug release behavior of Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD with different GQD concentrations in simulated gastrointestinal tract passage (pH and time).
Results of MTT assay on Caco-2 cells after 3 h incubation time with 0%, 10%, 20%, and 30% GQD containing Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD. Results are expressed as mean ± S.D. of three experiments.

head.ce:title: Cu-crosslinked carboxymethylcellulose/naproxen/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel beads for naproxen oral delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology (RCPN), Tabriz University of Medical Science, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, P.O. Box 51666, Tabriz, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Dendrimers and Nanopolymers

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, copper acetate was used as a new physical crosslinker to prepare carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) based hydrogel nanocomposite beads. Due to the characteristics of the prepared CMC-based hydrogel nanocomposite beads such as mildness, simplicity, and the creation of small and uniform shapes, the presented procedure could attract great consideration in the field of controlled release of drugs. Naproxen (NPX) as a model drug was pre-loaded during the preparation of hydrogel beads. The prepared Cu-crosslinked carboxymethylcellulose/NPX/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite hydrogel beads (Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD) characterized using FT-IR, Zeta potential, DSC and SEM analysis methods In order to demonstrate the efficiency of the prepared nanocomposite beads as a controlled drug delivery system, the drug delivery tests carried out in the gastrointestinal tract simulated conditions. The resulted drug release analysis showed that CMC could effectively protect the loaded drug against stomach pH. With controlling the releases in the gastrointestinal tract conditions, the long-term stability of drug dosing enhanced. The MTT test demonstrated that the hydrogel nanocomposite beads have low toxicity against Caco-2 cells. The obtained results showed that the prepared beads could be a choice for drug capsule in the gastrointestinal tract conditions; furthermore, it potentially could be used as an oral drug delivery system.

ce:section.ce:para: Control of drug release is of great importance in medicine; hence, it has received an increased interest in recent years (Ghorpade, Yadav, & Dias, 2017; Namazi, 2017; Yang, Chen, Pan, Wan, & Wang, 2013). The features such as biocompatibility, controlled biodegradability, inertness and high drug loading are the most important factors for controlled drug delivery systems (Chacko, Ventura, Zhuang, & Thayumanavan, 2012; Namazi & Hamrahloo, 2011; Yoo, Irvine, Discher, & Mitragotri, 2011). Organizing of drug release profiles with the aim of keeping the drug dose at a certain required level for a long time could prevent the side effects of the explosive drug release. Moreover, this could accelerate the treatment process and increase the injection time intervals (Naahidi et al., 2013; Namazi, Toomari, & Abbaspour, 2014; Tozaki et al., 2002). Thermodynamic and pH stability, easy fabrication and safety are some of the most important requirements of an ideal drug delivery vehicle (Elzoghby, Samy, & Elgindy, 2012; Hatefi & Amsden, 2002; Hu et al., 2014; Palazzo et al., 2007; Wang, Chen, Liu, Wu, & Guo, 2017). Currently, among various carriers for drug delivery system including polymeric particles (Chung, Lee, Neikirk, Nelson, & Priestley, 2012), nanoparticles (Zhang et al., 2012), dendrimers (Namazi & Adeli, 2005; Namazi & Heydari, 2014), microspheres (Son et al., 2011) and liposomes (Abraham et al., 2005), nanomaterials have demonstrated more advantages as drug carriers.

Graphene quantum dot nanoparticles (GQDs) have recently gained an increasing attention, because of their strong quantum confinement and edge effects (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017; Ritter & Lyding, 2009). Enormous efforts were devoted to the GQDs synthesis (bottom-up and top-down methods) and determination of their properties (Li et al., 2013; Sun, Wu, Wei, & Qu, 2013; Zhu, Tang, Zhang, & Yang, 2012), while their applications in biomedical field have not been extensively studied. GQDs appeared as promising materials for biomedical applications (Li et al., 2013; Ristic et al., 2014; Shen, Zhu, Yang, & Li, 2012; Wu et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2012). GQDs have high fundamental fluorescence, large surface area with delocalized electrons, unparalleled excitation relevant emission, solubility in the variety of solvents, chemical inertness, local functional groups at the edges, ability of drug loading by π-π interactions, and easy variability of size and shape. In addition, GQDs have been extensively investigated for cellular imaging and drug delivery (Li et al., 2013; Liu, Robinson, Sun, & Dai, 2008; Shen et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013; Zhu et al., 2011). The structure of GQDs are totally composed of “carbon” atoms, and they are nontoxic in comparison with inorganic quantum dots which probably have toxic metal ions in their residues after degradation (Akhavan, Ghaderi, & Akhavan, 2012; Chang et al., 2011; Qu, Wang, Wang, Liu, & Jiang, 2013; Shen et al., 2012).

CMC is a cellulose carboxymethyl derivation, which has unique characteristics such as pH-sensitivity, non-toxicity, hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, and gel-forming properties (Barkhordari, Yadollahi, & Namazi, 2014; Chen, Yao, Guan, & Lin, 2005; Tripathy & Raichur, 2013). CMC based hydrogels are widely used in versatile fields and it is a great candidate for biomedical applications (Rasoulzadeh & Namazi, 2017; Yadollahi, Farhoudian, & Namazi, 2015; Youssef, El-Sayed, El-Sayed, Salama, & Dufresne, 2016). CMC produces spherical beads in the presence of ionic crosslinkers (such as Al3+ and Fe3+). Features including simplicity, mildness, and uniform beads formation make CMC based bead as a great candidate for controlled drug delivery applications (Bhattacharya et al., 2013; Wang, Zhang, Tan, Liu, & Shi, 2011). All of the available CMC physical crosslinkers like aluminum chloride and iron (III) chloride create highly acidic solutions that makes them unsuitable for pH-sensitive drugs. In the present work, for the first time, we have introduced copper acetate as a new CMC crosslinker. Since the pH of its solution is not so acidic, therefore, it could be used for the pH-sensitive drugs.

NPX ((S)-6-methoxy-a-methyl-2-naphthaleneacetic acid) is an commercially available and effective nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug for rheumatoid arthritis, colitis, and osteoarthritis treatment (Espinar, Igea, Mendez, & Jato, 1991). One of the major problems of NPX oral administration is the gastrointestinal tract (GIT) irritation (Parfitt, 1999). An effective solution for this problem could be encapsulation of NPX into biocompatible polymeric hydrogel supports.

As a part of our ongoing program about the developing new drug delivery systems (Namazi, Rakhshaei, Hamishehkar, & Kafil, 2016) and based on our previous investigations on CMC (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2018; Rasoulzadeh & Namazi, 2017), we have reported the copper acetate as a new CMC crosslinker. The pH of the copper acetate solution is not so acidic and it could be utilized with pH-sensitive drugs. Moreover, GQD nanoparticles were added to the prepared hydrogel to investigate their effects on physicochemical properties of the hydrogel and NPX release in the simulated gastrointestinal tract conditions.

CMC with a degree of substitution about 0.55–1.0 and viscosity of 15,000 mPas/s (1% in H2O, 25 °C) purchased from Nippon Paper Industries (Japan). Citric acid (CA), Copper (II) acetate (Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O), aluminum (III) chloride (AlCl3·6H2O), and iron (III) chloride (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich Co. All solvents used for the experiments were of analytical grade and used without further purification. NPX (98% purity) was obtained from Danna Pharma Co. (Tabriz, Iran). Double distilled water used in all steps of this work and all other materials purchased from Merck Co.

UV–vis absorption spectra obtained on a Shimadzu 1700 Model UV–vis spectrophotometer. The Fourier Transform Infrared spectra were recorded with Bruker Instruments, model Aquinox 55, Germany in the 4000–400 cm−1 range at a resolution of 0.5 cm−1 as KBr pellets. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) equipped with Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectroscopy (MIRA3 FEG-SEM Tescan, Czech) used to determine the surface morphology and EDX of the samples. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements were obtained with a DLS-ZP/Particle Sizer (Microtrac, model Nanotrac Wave). Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis of the materials was performed using DSC (Linseis-L63/45, Germany) at 10 °C/min heating rate in the temperature range of 25–350 °C.

The GQDs were prepared in accordance with previous reports via direct pyrolysis of CA (Dong et al., 2012). In a typical procedure, 2 g CA was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated up to 200 °C using a heating mantle. About 30 min later, the obtained orange liquid was added dropwise into 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution, under vigorous stirring. After neutralizing with NaOH, the prepared GQD was dried and then pulverized.

Firstly, to prepare nanocomposite containing 10%, 20%, and 30% GQDs, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 mg of the prepared GQDs dissolved in 100 mL water. In the second step, 0.1 g NPX was added to the every initial solution. Then 1 g CMC gradually added to the all three solutions. To prepare pure CMC/NPX bead, CMC and NPX added in 100 mL water. The mixture stirred for 12 h at room temperature on the stirrer to ensure the complete dispersion of GQDs and NPX among the polymer chains. Finally, the prepared mixture added dropwise to a 100 mL of Copper (II) acetate solution (0.01 M) as cross-linker. The prepared Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD washed three times with water and dried at room temperature.

The in-vitro swelling tests carried out in a simulated gastrointestinal tract conditions as previously reported with some modifications (Barkhordari & Yadollahi, 2016; Sgorla et al., 2018; Xie, Yin, Liu, Zhu, & Yang, 2018). To determine the swelling percentage, the prepared beads were consecutively immersed in 10 mL of buffered solutions with pH 1.2 (for 2 h), 6.8 (for 2 h), and 7.4 (for 4 h). At the certain time intervals, the swelled hydrogel nanocomposite beads were taken out, blotted with filter paper to remove excess water, and weighed. The swelling percentage calculated with Eq. (1):
(1)
S w e l l i n g
R a t e =
(
W s − W d
)
W d
Where, Wd and Ws are the weights of the dry and swollen samples, respectively.

The in-vitro drug release tests carried out in a simulated gastrointestinal tract conditions as previously reported with some modifications (Barkhordari & Yadollahi, 2016; Sgorla et al., 2018; Xie et al., 2018). To determine in-vitro drug release, the prepared beads were consecutively immersed in 10 mL of buffered solutions with pH 1.2 (for 2 h), 6.8 (for 2 h), and 7.4 (for 4 h). At certain time intervals, adequate amounts of samples picked up and in order to maintain the volume of buffer in a constant value, same amount of fresh buffer was replaced. The amount of releasing drug was measured using UV–vis spectrophotometer at λ = 230 nm. The percent of released drug was determined using the following Eq. (2):
(2)
D r u g
r e l e a s e =
t h e
a m o u n t
o f
r e l e a s e d
d r u g
t h e
a m o u n t
o f
l o a d e d
d r u g
× 100

MTT assay was used to evaluate the biocompatibility of the prepared Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel bead (Shahnaz, Perera, Sakloetsakun, Rahmat, & Bernkop-Schnürch, 2010). In summary, Caco-2 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells/well in 100 μL cell medium and incubated overnight. Then cell monolayers were washed with 1 × PBS. Negative control and positive control test solutions (1%) were respectively added and incubated for 3 h. The number of cells was manually determined using a haemacytometer. Afterward, cells treated with Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD. 1 mL of 0.5 mg/mL of MTT solution (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) was added to each well and plates were incubated at 37 °C for 1.5 h. The culture medium removed after 4 h and then 1 mL of HCl in isopropanol (0.04 M) was added to solubilize the formazan crystals. Culturing experiments were repeated three times.

The SEM image of the synthesized GQDs nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 1 a. Uniform spherical particles with a narrow distribution of particle size in the range of 4–6 nm are visible and confirmed using DLS experiment (Fig. 1b). A sharp absorption band with a narrow full width at 362 nm (Fig. 1c) confined sp2 clusters in GQDs should be uniform in size. The excitation peak of GQDs is almost the same as the wavelength of the absorption peak of GQDs and their maximum emission wavelength found at 460 nm (Fig. 1d). The presence of only carbon, oxygen and sodium in EDX analysis proved the successful formation and high purity of GQDs (Zhang, Gao, Niu, & Liu, 2014).

Fig. 2 shows FT-IR spectra of CA, GQDs, pure CMC, NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD. The GQDs showed absorption of stretching vibration of CH in the range lower 1350 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH at 2950 cm−1, suggesting the GQDs contain some incompletely carbonized CA. As well as, the skeletal vibration of aromatic rings around 1450–1650 cm−1 and stretching vibration of CH in aromatic rings around 3000–3100 cm−1 were detected. In the CMC FT-IR spectrum, the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups were observed around the 3375 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of CH2 is located at 2930 cm−1. NPX FT-IR spectrum presents the CH band at 2900 cm−1 and the CO band at 1695 cm−1. The FT-IR spectrum of Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD is a combination of NPX, GQDs and CMC FT-IR spectra that have characteristic bands related to the NPX, GQDs, and CMC. The Cu-O vibration peaks from 400 to 500 cm−1 could be related to the physically cross-linking of CMC with Cu2+ (Farhoudian, Yadollahi, & Namazi, 2016).

The DSC thermograms of the pure and cross-linked CMC are presented in Fig. 3 a. The thermogram of CMC showed a broad endothermic peak at 130.5 °C and two exothermic peaks at 289 °C and 301 °C. Whereas Cu-CMC showed an endothermic and tow exothermic peaks at 120.5 °C, 225 °C and 263 °C, respectively. The observed early stage peaks are relevant to the loss of water while the observed peaks at higher temperatures on DSC thermograms of the samples is related to either their melting point or oxidative degradation point. The observed differences could be attributed to the change in polymeric network. The shifting of the endothermic peak at early stage temperatures and broadening of the exothermic peaks demonstrate chemical modifications in the polymeric network due to higher crosslinking density (Malik, Ahmad, & Minhas, 2017; Park, Park, & Ruckenstein, 2001).

The surficial charges of the Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD were characterized using Zeta potential test (Fig. 3a). Results proved that the positive surficial charges of Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD in the acidic pH were changed to negative at elevated pH. The pKa of the CO2H functional groups on available on the CMC chains is about 4.6 and they are partially were protonated at pH 1.2. With increasing of the pH to 6.8, the positive surficial charge of nanocomposite gradually changed to neutral. With increasing the pH to 7.4, the CO2H functional groups on the CMC chains, NPX and GQD were deprotonated and the surficial charge changed to negative (Lv et al., 2016; Tarvainen et al., 2006).

One of the most important factors affecting the application of the hydrogels is the swelling percentage (Jayaramudu et al., 2017). The swelling amount is dependent on the several parameters such as composition, pH, temperature, ionic strength, and extent of crosslinking in the network (Varaprasad, Jayaramudu, & Sadiku, 2017). At the pH 1.2, the carboxylic groups of the CMC are protonated which was proved by the Zeta potential data (Fig. 3a). Under these conditions, the hydrogen-bonding interactions is generated among carboxylic acid groups. Hydrogen-bonding is a physical crosslinker, so the network tends to shrink, and as a result, the swelling values are decreased (Fig. 5 ). Unlike acidic medium, by increasing the pH up to 7.4 some of the carboxylic groups in the CMC network deprotonate resulting to the electrostatic repulsion between carboxylate anions and the increase of swelling degree compared to pH 1.2. As well as, nanocomposite beads showed a GQD-dependent swelling property. By increasing the GQD content in the nanocomposite beads, the swelling ratio was dramatically increased that may be is related to the negative surficial charge of GQD nanoparticles (zeta potential −9.6 mV) (Lv et al., 2016) resulting to more electrostatic repulsion between carboxylate anions of CMC and GQDs.

To explain the potential of the prepared beads as an oral drug-delivery system, drug release experiments carried out in simulated conditions with the in-vivo process. For investigation, during several specific period of time, the beads were consecutively suspended in the medium with pH 1.2, 6.8, and 7.4. Fig. 6 illustrates the drug release profiles of the Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD with different GQD contents after 8 h at 37 °C. Comparing the diagrams, the drug release in the first zone (pH 1.2), has the lowest drug release rate for all of the nanocomposite beads. After that, the release rate increased for all of the nanocomposite beads. This might be related to the pH-sensitive nature of CMC polymer and the diffusion barrier of the beads (Barkhordari & Yadollahi, 2016).

Moreover, the release percentage of NPX decreased by increasing GQD concentration. It expect due to the increase of the swelling rate of nanocomposite beads by increasing GQD concentration, the release percentage must be decreased while that was not observed. This showed the interaction between the drug and nanoparticle plays an important role in the release process.

Cytotoxicity of the Cu-CMC/NPX and Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD was calculated using the MTT assay (Fig. 7 ). After 3 h incubation, the nanocomposite demonstrated low cytotoxicity to Caco-2 cells and the cell viability remained above 90%. Similar results were reported by previous studies (Shahnaz et al., 2010). The cell viability values of the 0%, 10%, 20%, and 30% GQD containing Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD were 90.96, 92.64, 93.70 and 94.15, respectively. The GQD nanoparticles were definitely useful for improving the biocompatibility of Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD. Therefore, for oral delivery system, these nanocomposites could be potentially a safe carrier.

In this work, in the first step, GQD was prepared and characterized. The DLS, FT-IR, UV–vis, EDX and PL spectra proved preparation of GQDs. Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD drug carrier prepared through the pre-loading of the NPX drug during the preparation of hydrogel beads. Furthermore, the FT-IR analysis of Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD confirmed the existence of GQDs and NPX within the CMC biopolymer matrix. As a great advantage over the availabe conventional cross-linkers like AlCl3, the pH of it’s copper acetate solution is not so acidic and suitable for pH-sensitive drugs. The NPX release studies showed a long time, controlled release and high stability of drug dosing in the intestinal tract conditions for Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD. Cytotoxicity results exhibited a low toxicity of prepared nanocomposite against Caco-2 cells. The obtained results showed that the prepared Cu-CMC/NPX/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel beads have the potential to use as advanced pH-sensitive drug delivery system for the oral administration of NPX.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the University of Tabriz (Grant Number 949645105) and Research Center for Pharmaceutical Nanotechnology, the Tabriz University of Medical Science for their financial support of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2018.04.103.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
